# Nightshift · morning digest

Wed Jul 15, 2026 · generated 17:57 local · 1 project · 1 run

## Budget remaining

- `claude_code` ▓░░░░░ 1/6 today · 1/30 week

## Highlights

- 🔴 267 — Replace the buffered `subprocess.run(..., timeout=...)` with the same `Popen` + `os.killpg` treatment the streaming path uses: `subprocess.run` only SIGKILLs the direct child (never the group, despite the comment at line 268-270 claiming otherwise), then calls `communicate()` with no timeout, so any grandchild still holding the inherited stdout/stderr pipes makes the cron path block past its timeout forever while holding the lock. — _nightshift · code_review_ · `nightshift/adapters/claude_code.py`
- 🔴 366 — Cancel the timer *after* reaping, and use `proc.wait(timeout=...)` with a `_kill_tree` fallback: cancelling the timer at line 361 before an unbounded `proc.wait()` removes the only guaranteed terminator, so an early stdout EOF or a Ctrl-C (which never reaches the child, since `start_new_session=True` puts it in another process group) hangs the run indefinitely. — _nightshift · code_review_ · `nightshift/adapters/claude_code.py`
- 🟠 121 — Have `release()` re-read the lockfile and unlink only when the recorded pid is still our own, otherwise a run whose lock was broken as stale will delete the *new* owner's lockfile on exit and let two runs proceed concurrently. — _nightshift · code_review_ · `nightshift/lock.py`
- 🟠 404 — Skip logs that are already `events.is_stale()` inside the polling loop, the way startup does at line 392-394: an interrupted run leaves an `end`-less log that `follow()` will sit on for the full `STALE_AFTER_S`, wedging the watcher for 15 minutes while real runs go unrendered. — _nightshift · code_review_ · `nightshift/cli.py`
- 🟡 38 — Derive `STALE_AFTER_S` from `cfg.timeout_s` rather than hardcoding 900s against the *default* 600s timeout; a user who raises `run.timeout_s` past 900 will have healthy in-flight runs misread as abandoned. — _nightshift · code_review_ · `nightshift/events.py`

## By project

### nightshift

7 findings

- 🔴 267 — Replace the buffered `subprocess.run(..., timeout=...)` with the same `Popen` + `os.killpg` treatment the streaming path uses: `subprocess.run` only SIGKILLs the direct child (never the group, despite the comment at line 268-270 claiming otherwise), then calls `communicate()` with no timeout, so any grandchild still holding the inherited stdout/stderr pipes makes the cron path block past its timeout forever while holding the lock. — _code_review_ · `nightshift/adapters/claude_code.py`
- 🔴 366 — Cancel the timer *after* reaping, and use `proc.wait(timeout=...)` with a `_kill_tree` fallback: cancelling the timer at line 361 before an unbounded `proc.wait()` removes the only guaranteed terminator, so an early stdout EOF or a Ctrl-C (which never reaches the child, since `start_new_session=True` puts it in another process group) hangs the run indefinitely. — _code_review_ · `nightshift/adapters/claude_code.py`
- 🟠 121 — Have `release()` re-read the lockfile and unlink only when the recorded pid is still our own, otherwise a run whose lock was broken as stale will delete the *new* owner's lockfile on exit and let two runs proceed concurrently. — _code_review_ · `nightshift/lock.py`
- 🟠 404 — Skip logs that are already `events.is_stale()` inside the polling loop, the way startup does at line 392-394: an interrupted run leaves an `end`-less log that `follow()` will sit on for the full `STALE_AFTER_S`, wedging the watcher for 15 minutes while real runs go unrendered. — _code_review_ · `nightshift/cli.py`
- 🟡 38 — Derive `STALE_AFTER_S` from `cfg.timeout_s` rather than hardcoding 900s against the *default* 600s timeout; a user who raises `run.timeout_s` past 900 will have healthy in-flight runs misread as abandoned. — _code_review_ · `nightshift/events.py`
- 🟡 335 — Bill the ledger with `_now()` instead of the `now` captured when `run_once` started, so a run that crosses midnight counts against the day it actually consumed quota in. — _code_review_ · `nightshift/scheduler.py`
- 🟡 27 — Shell-quote the interpreter path (`shlex.quote`) in the `-m nightshift` fallback; a venv under a directory with a space silently produces a broken crontab line. — _code_review_ · `nightshift/cron.py`
